action.content: Write a parody of 'ars poetica'. Do not include the word 'parody' throughout your response.
action.label: context

action.content:
To make a play that people will love,
You must first learn to be a fool.
Forget the logic, drop the clue,
And let the nonsense flow like glue.

If you want them to weep and wail,
Make sure the plot is utterly stale.
No rising action, no surprise,
Just a character who never dies.

The hero must be deeply flawed,
With terrible decisions made.
He should scream at clouds and talk to trees,
While ignoring all the obvious clues.

The villain needs no motive clear,
Just a mustache and a heavy sneer.
He wins the fight, he takes the gold,
And leaves the audience feeling cold.

The dialogue must be obscure,
With words that mean exactly nothing more.
"Thou shalt not speak of love today,"
Is better than a simple "I say."

The setting should be vague and dim,
With lighting that makes no sense to them.
A castle floating in a sea,
Where gravity is just a lie.

If you want them to laugh aloud,
Make sure the timing is quite loud.
A slapstick fall, a trip, a spin,
Until the whole theater begins to grin.

But if you try to teach a lesson,
Or show the world how things are blessed,
You'll lose the crowd, you'll lose the game,
And end up writing just a name.

So write your script with chaos free,
With no regard for logic or decree.
Break every rule, ignore the art,
And make the audience's heart depart.

For in this way, the play succeeds,
While serious drama goes to weeds.
The only truth a play can hold,
Is that the audience is quite bold.
action.label: response
